items.text: When the Remote Action Gateway receives a LOCK event, it SHALL terminate all in-progress SSH sessions within 1 s, reject all new action requests, and return a deny envelope with status 'LOCKED' until an UNLOCK event is received.
items.rationale: Derived from SYS-REQ-003 (lock safe-state within 1 s). Active SSH sessions executing on remote hosts must be terminated on lock to prevent orphaned command execution after the operator has secured the host. The 1 s budget aligns with the system-level lock requirement.

items.text: The Remote Action Gateway's Remote Action Result Emitter SHALL encode each result as a JSON envelope containing: status (permit|deny|error|timeout), target_host, command, stdout, stderr, exit_code, duration_ms, and an ISO 8601 timestamp, and SHALL emit the envelope to the Intent Router within 100 ms of receiving the completed result from the SSH Session Manager.
items.rationale: Derived from SYS-REQ-009 (reflect execution to desklet) and FN-FN-013 (report action outcome). The typed fields allow the Intent Router and desklet client to distinguish policy denials from SSH errors from application exits, enabling correct UI feedback and audit records for each case.

items.text: The Remote Action Gateway's SSH Session Manager SHALL enforce a command-class execution policy: for bounded commands (service status, log tail, restart, health check), the Session Manager SHALL terminate the SSH session and return a TIMEOUT result envelope (exit_code = -1, error = 'TIMEOUT') after 30 s; for long-running commands (deploy, backup), the Session Manager SHALL use an async streaming execution path with no fixed timeout ceiling, streaming stdout/stderr output via IFC-RAG-026 and terminating only on remote process exit or operator cancel.
items.rationale: SUB-RSB-061 defines the command-class split at the Remote SSH Boundary; the SSH Session Manager must implement the same split because it is the component that actually executes SSH commands (per SUB-EXE-026 routing). A flat 30-second ceiling on the Session Manager defeats the SUB-RSB-061 async exemption for deploy and backup, which routinely exceed 30 s. The class split preserves the resource-bound rationale (30 s ceiling on bounded commands) while making the deploy and backup use-cases in STK-REQ-009 and IFC-REQ-004 operable.

items.text: The Remote Action Gateway's SSH Session Manager SHALL use only the host operator's existing SSH configuration (sourced from ~/.ssh/config, ~/.ssh/known_hosts, and identity files on the host filesystem) and SHALL NOT accept, store, or transmit SSH credentials from any desklet or external input.
items.rationale: Derived from SYS-REQ-001 (credentials on host only) and SYS-REQ-015 (use existing SSH config). Restricting to the host operator's existing SSH configuration ensures the Gateway cannot be used to introduce new credential material from untrusted sources, and aligns with the principle that all credential management remains on the host.

items.text: The Remote Action Gateway SHALL validate every incoming remote SSH action request against the active allowlist policy via the Allowlist Gate before dispatching to the SSH Session Manager, and SHALL reject any request not matched by a permit rule with a deny response containing the policy rule identifier within 50 ms.
items.rationale: Directly derives from SYS-REQ-004 (allowlist gate) and SYS-REQ-015 (SSH via allowlist gate). The 50 ms rejection latency is required for responsive UI feedback; delayed rejections cause the desklet to show ambiguous pending state. Policy rule ID in the response enables audit correlation.

items.text: The Heartbeat Monitor SHALL send a liveness probe to each registered desklet at a 1 s interval and SHALL declare a desklet link-lost if no acknowledgement is received within 5 s, emitting a DeskletLinkLost event and updating the Device Identity Ledger last-seen timestamp on each successful response.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-008 requires link-loss detection within 5 s via heartbeat. The 1 s probe interval with a 5 s failure window provides 5 consecutive missed probes before declaration, tolerating transient network jitter without false positives while meeting the SYS-level timing constraint. Updating last-seen on success enables diagnostic reporting of stale connections.

items.text: The Role Assignment Manager SHALL bind each paired desklet to exactly one role from the set {Project, Actions, Status, Capture, Logs, AI}, SHALL reject binding requests for roles outside this set with a typed error, and SHALL notify the Workspace Context Store subsystem within 200 ms of a role assignment or change.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-007 defines the canonical desklet role set as {Project, Actions, Status, Capture, Logs, AI}. Rejection of out-of-set roles prevents configuration errors from silently producing undefined context projections. The 200 ms notification window ensures the Role Scope Filter updates its projection mask before the next streaming cycle.

items.text: The Device Identity Ledger SHALL persist pairing records (device identifier, pairing timestamp, assigned role, last-seen timestamp) across host restarts, SHALL support a maximum of 8 concurrent registered desklets, and SHALL provide a read-stable query path that does not block on concurrent write operations.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-008 requires heartbeat-based reconnect which depends on a persistent identity list. Persisting across restarts means a desklet reconnecting after a host reboot is recognised without re-pairing, critical for the Degraded mode defined in SYS-REQ-013. The 8-device ceiling matches the deployment scenario cap and bounds storage requirements. Read-stable query path required by the Heartbeat Monitor design decision (ARC).

items.text: The Pairing Token Generator SHALL generate a cryptographically random single-use pairing token of at least 128 bits entropy, encode it as a QR code, expire the token after 30 s if unused, and invalidate it immediately upon first authenticated use, permitting only one outstanding token at a time.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-002 requires single-use token authentication. 128-bit entropy prevents brute-force scanning within the 30 s window at any realistic QR-scan rate. One-at-a-time token issuance prevents simultaneous multi-device pairing races that could bind two desklets to the same identity slot.

items.text: When the Desklet Client Framework process on the Knowledge Worker Desklet Station crashes or becomes unresponsive, the desklet host SHALL provide fault recovery by restarting it within 10 s, restoring the last assigned role and last received context snapshot without requiring the user to re-pair (session failover), and displaying a reconnecting indicator during the restart interval.
items.rationale: The Knowledge Worker Desklet Station (UHT: System-Essential, hex 40AD1888) is the user's sole interface to workspace context; a crash with no recovery path would sever the knowledge worker from their role surface entirely, violating SYS-REQ-008 which requires desklet-link loss to be recovered with prior role restored. Automatic restart with role/context restoration avoids the manual re-pair overhead that would interrupt knowledge-work flow.

items.text: The Knowledge Worker Desklet Station SHALL display a staleness indicator on the role context view when no context update has been received for more than 10 s, and SHALL attempt WebSocket reconnection within 5 s of connection loss.
items.rationale: 10-second staleness threshold matches the heartbeat detection window from SYS-REQ-008; a visible staleness indicator prevents the Knowledge Worker from acting on outdated context without realising the connection is lost. The 5-second reconnect window ensures desklet availability resumes promptly after transient link failures.

items.text: While the Intent Router is active, the Intent Dispatcher SHALL NOT forward any intent to a downstream executor unless the ACG Policy Engine has issued an explicit PERMIT decision for that intent, constituting the human-governed interlock that bounds the Intent Router's autonomous dispatch behaviour and SHALL cease dispatching to any executor that returns a consecutive run of FAILURE outcomes exceeding the operator-configured failure threshold.
items.rationale: UHT trait Functionally Autonomous (bit 15) on the Intent Router requires an explicit override/safety constraint; the ACG gate is the interlock (human-configurable via the allowlist) and the consecutive-failure stop is the fail-safe, together ensuring no autonomous runaway dispatch occurs without operator-sanctioned permission.

items.text: The Conflict Serialiser SHALL maintain a per-context-object intent queue and SHALL apply concurrent intents targeting the same context object in receipt order, emitting an explicit SUPERSEDED status event for any intent that is displaced by a later-queued intent from a different desklet before the earlier intent is dispatched.
items.rationale: Realises SYS-REQ-010; a dedicated per-context-object serialisation queue with explicit SUPERSEDED events prevents data races on the workspace context model while allowing uncontested intents on different context objects to proceed concurrently, preserving throughput.

items.text: When a Knowledge Worker dispatches an intent from a desklet, the Dispatch Confirmer SHALL deliver an execution outcome event (SUCCESS, FAILURE, or TIMEOUT) to the originating desklet within 200 ms of the moment the intent is received at the Intent Dispatcher, and SHALL emit a TIMEOUT event proactively if no downstream acknowledgment is received within the latency budget.
items.rationale: Realises SYS-REQ-009; the Dispatch Confirmer owns the latency obligation end-to-end — decoupling confirmation tracking from the dispatcher allows proactive TIMEOUT emission independent of whether the executor responds, preventing silent failures that would leave the desklet waiting indefinitely.

items.text: The Intent Dispatcher SHALL verify the presence of an ACG-issued permission record on each inbound intent before dispatching it to the Workspace Executor or Remote Action Gateway, and SHALL reject any intent lacking a valid permission record with a PERMISSION_DENIED status without forwarding it to any downstream component.
items.rationale: Realises SYS-REQ-007; the Intent Dispatcher is the internal execution boundary where ACG-validated role-capability enforcement is confirmed — no intent may cross to an executor without a verifiable permission record, preventing role bypasses that could arise from routing errors.

items.text: The Configuration Loader SHALL validate the ContextRail configuration file against the embedded JSON Schema specification at startup and SHALL halt the boot sequence with a structured error message identifying the failing schema path if any validation constraint is violated.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-016 requires all core functions operate without outbound internet connectivity; the configuration schema embeds adapter registry and transport parameters that must be correct at boot. A schema violation allowed to pass silently would cause runtime failures that cannot be diagnosed without network access to external documentation.

items.text: The Startup Orchestrator SHALL start all ContextRail subsystems in the following dependency order: (1) Configuration Loader, (2) Workspace Context Store, (3) Security and Lock Manager, (4) Allowlist and Configuration Governance, (5) Local Transport Server, (6) Adapter Framework, (7) Intent Router; and SHALL abort startup and emit BOOT_FAILED if any subsystem does not signal READY within 5 s of being started.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-001 requires that all credential handling and sensitive actions be within the host process; the dependency order ensures Security and Lock Manager is enforcing access control before the transport layer admits any desklet connection, preventing a race condition where a desklet could connect before authentication is active.

items.text: The Health Watchdog SHALL poll each registered subsystem heartbeat endpoint at intervals of 2 s ± 100 ms and SHALL trigger a SUBSYSTEM_FAILED event to the Mode State Machine after 2 consecutive missed heartbeats from the same subsystem (total detection window ≤5 s).
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-008 requires desklet link loss detection within 5s; the Health Watchdog detection window is set to ≤5s (2 polls at 2s each) to stay within that budget. A single missed heartbeat is not treated as failure to reduce false positives from transient scheduling jitter on the host OS.

items.text: While in Degraded mode, the Mode State Machine SHALL broadcast a MODE_DEGRADED event to all registered subsystems within 500 ms of receiving a SUBSYSTEM_FAILED trigger from the Health Watchdog, and SHALL include the identity of the failed subsystem in the event payload.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-013 requires that surviving desklets continue rendering last-known context with staleness <30s; the 500ms broadcast budget is derived from the 200ms propagation budget of SYS-REQ-012 leaving 300ms for the Workspace Context Store to enter read-only mode and freeze its snapshot.

items.text: The Mode State Machine SHALL enforce a three-state operating model (Nominal, Degraded, Maintenance) and SHALL reject any state transition not defined in the valid transition graph, logging the rejected transition source, target, and trigger event.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-013 mandates Degraded-mode operation with specific capability constraints; an explicit state machine with a validated transition graph prevents illegal mode jumps (e.g., Maintenance → Degraded without operator action) that could expose allowlist configuration during a subsystem failure.

items.text: When the Host Administration Station client session is unavailable or unreachable, the ContextRail Host Core Runtime SHALL maintain fault-tolerant operation in the last committed allowlist and lock configuration for an indefinite period, accept no new configuration changes (failover-hold posture), and log a WARN event every 60 s until a valid HAS session is re-established.
items.rationale: The Host Administration Station (UHT: System-Essential, hex 50A53888) is the sole configuration path for allowlist changes; treating its absence as a hard fault would cause the host to halt legitimate knowledge-worker operations. Operating in last-committed configuration satisfies the SYS-REQ-016 continuous-availability requirement and ensures the security posture is never silently changed by HAS unavailability.

items.text: The Host Administration Station SHALL permit allowlist modification commands only while the ContextRail host is in Maintenance mode, and SHALL reject modification requests in all other modes with a descriptive error message.
items.rationale: Derived from SYS-REQ-005 (allowlist entries may only be modified via the host while in Maintenance mode); this prevents an operator in Operational mode from inadvertently modifying policy affecting active desklet sessions.

items.text: The Command Dispatcher SHALL route a CommandEnvelope to the correct execution path based on the adapter ID field: an empty or 'local' adapter ID SHALL invoke the Process Supervisor directly; a 'rag' adapter ID SHALL forward the envelope to the Remote Action Gateway via IFC-RAG-026; any other adapter ID SHALL route through the Adapter Framework broker.
items.rationale: Three distinct execution paths exist in ContextRail (local subprocess, remote SSH, and external adapter) and the Command Dispatcher is the single routing authority. Hardcoding the adapter-ID routing table in the Dispatcher rather than discovering it dynamically avoids race conditions during startup when adapters may not yet be registered, and keeps the routing logic auditable from a single component.

items.text: The Command Dispatcher SHALL process CommandEnvelope messages sequentially in receipt order; no two subprocess executions SHALL be active simultaneously within the Workspace Executor; a CommandEnvelope received while a subprocess is executing SHALL be queued and processed after the current execution completes.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-010 requires intent serialisation for context objects; the Workspace Executor enforces this at the execution layer by permitting only one subprocess at a time. Concurrent subprocess execution would produce non-deterministic interleaving of filesystem and application state mutations, making the workspace context model inconsistent and violating the deterministic sequencing guarantee.

items.text: The Outcome Reporter SHALL publish a CommandOutcome event to the Workspace Context Store event bus within 10 ms of receiving the ProcessResult from the Process Supervisor; the CommandOutcome SHALL include the intent ID, status (SUCCESS, FAILURE, TIMEOUT, DENIED), exit code, stdout digest, truncation flag, and elapsed milliseconds.
items.rationale: IFC-EXE-018 requires CommandOutcome delivery within 50 ms of command completion. The 10 ms budget allocated to the Outcome Reporter is the per-stage slice of that 50 ms window, leaving margin for event bus scheduling jitter. The structured fields enable the Workspace Context Store to update the context object without storing full command output, and allow the Intent Router to return the CommandResult to the originating desklet with audit fields intact.

items.text: The Process Supervisor SHALL capture the first 4 KB of combined stdout and stderr from every subprocess, compute a SHA-256 digest of the captured output, and pass the digest and truncation flag to the Outcome Reporter; raw output beyond 4 KB SHALL be discarded.
items.rationale: The CommandOutcome event (IFC-EXE-018) carries a stdout digest rather than raw output to bound event bus message size. 4 KB is the maximum output that can traverse the in-process event bus without risk of back-pressure on the Context Store subscriber; the digest allows downstream consumers to detect output changes without storing full output on the event bus.

items.text: The Process Supervisor SHALL acknowledge each intent with an execution-started confirmation within 200 ms of receipt; the Process Supervisor SHALL enforce a 5-second wall-clock execution timeout on every local subprocess and, when the subprocess has not exited within 5 s, SHALL deliver SIGKILL to the process group, collect the exit state, and yield a CommandResult with status TIMEOUT to the Outcome Reporter.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-009 bounds the end-to-end intent-to-outcome latency at 200 ms p95 for the acknowledgment path; this is a UI-responsiveness constraint on the confirmation signal, not on subprocess execution duration. Local scripts (file moves, app launches, workspace restores) may legitimately require several seconds to complete; a 200 ms hard kill would abort all non-trivial local actions, making local intent execution inoperable. The 5-second execution timeout is derived from the local-action use-case class: actions in the allowlisted set (launch, open URL, script, keyboard simulation, workspace restore) complete well within 5 s under normal conditions; SIGKILL to the process group prevents orphan children from accumulating when a script hangs.

items.text: The Command Dispatcher SHALL reject any CommandEnvelope whose action identifier is not backed by a current PERMIT record issued by the Policy Engine, and SHALL return a CommandResult with status DENIED and no process invocation within 5 ms of receipt.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-001 mandates that no sensitive action executes without host-side governance. The 5 ms bound keeps the permit check off the critical 200 ms path (SYS-REQ-009) while ensuring the denial is synchronous and auditable before any subprocess is spawned. A permit validation failure that results in a process execution is a security regression.

items.text: The External Application Boundary SHALL dispatch BASIC adapter actions only to local-network callback URLs (RFC 1918 address space) and SHALL return a failure result without dispatching if the target URL resolves to a non-local address.
items.rationale: Confining BASIC adapter callbacks to RFC 1918 addresses enforces SYS-REQ-001 (all sensitive actions inside the host process boundary) and prevents adapters from exfiltrating action payloads to external internet endpoints.

items.text: The Desklet Web Boundary SHALL relay role-scoped context updates to each connected desklet within 50 ms of context change publication, and SHALL enforce role-scope filtering so no context object outside the desklet's assigned role is transmitted.
items.rationale: 50 ms relay latency derives from SYS-REQ-012 (context propagation to all subscribers); role-scope filtering enforces SYS-REQ-011 (stream only the subset scoped to the desklet's role) and is a security boundary, not a quality-of-service feature.

items.text: The Desklet Web Boundary SHALL admit a desklet WebSocket connection only after validating the session token against the Device Identity Ledger, and SHALL reject unauthenticated upgrade requests with HTTP 403 within 200 ms.
items.rationale: Derived from SYS-REQ-002 (host SHALL authenticate desklet with a single-use pairing token); the 200 ms rejection latency ensures prompt feedback to a misconfigured desklet without blocking other connection attempts.

items.text: The Deep Integration Boundary SHALL deny and log every action request or event subscription from a DEEP adapter that exceeds the adapter's registered capability scope, and SHALL update enforced scopes within 2 s of an allowlist policy change.
items.rationale: Immediate scope denial enforces SYS-REQ-004 (allowlist evaluation for all host-mediated actions) at the DEEP adapter layer; the 2-second policy propagation window ensures no more than one policy cycle elapses before updated policy is in effect, bounding the window of stale-policy exposure.

items.text: When a DEEP adapter session heartbeat is not received within 10 s, the Deep Integration Boundary SHALL terminate the session, release all subscriptions, and notify the Adapter Framework of session loss within 1 s of timeout detection.
items.rationale: 10-second heartbeat timeout matches the desklet liveness window from SYS-REQ-008; consistent timeout values across boundaries simplify operator diagnostics. The 1-second notification window ensures the Adapter Framework can suppress further action routing to the lost adapter before the next intent arrives.

items.text: The Deep Integration Boundary SHALL route workspace context events to each subscribed DEEP adapter within 50 ms of event publication on the event bus, and SHALL rate-limit delivery to any single adapter to 100 events per second.
items.rationale: 50 ms delivery latency matches the context propagation SLA from SYS-REQ-012 (context change to all subscribers); the 100 events/s cap prevents a misbehaving adapter from saturating the event bus and degrading context delivery to desklets.

items.text: The Deep Integration Boundary SHALL accept DEEP adapter connections over a Unix domain socket and SHALL complete the capability-grant handshake within 500 ms of connection.
items.rationale: 500 ms handshake bound is derived from the 1-second adapter-start budget visible to the host operator; exceeding it causes the host to log an adapter startup warning. Unix domain socket is mandated by the ARC decision to avoid port conflicts and restrict to local processes.

items.text: The Context Feed Client SHALL process incoming role-scoped context updates at up to 10 Hz and deliver each update to the Desklet Shell Renderer within 50ms of receipt.
items.rationale: 10 Hz matches the maximum context publication rate of the Workspace Context Store. The 50ms in-process delivery budget ensures context-driven UI changes appear within one animation frame of the update arriving at the device, maintaining perceived immediacy.

items.text: The Connection Health Monitor SHALL detect WebSocket disconnection from the host within 10s and notify the Lifecycle State Machine to initiate reconnection with exponential back-off capped at 30s.
items.rationale: A 10s detection window balances responsiveness against false positives from transient network events. The 30s backoff cap ensures the desklet does not remain indefinitely disconnected in the event of a host restart or network partition.

items.text: The Desklet Client Framework SHALL resume normal rendering within 3s of receiving a resume command from the host, re-subscribing to the context feed and restoring the last assigned role display.
items.rationale: 3s budget for resume accounts for WebSocket reconnect (up to 2s on a congested LAN) plus one context snapshot delivery and render cycle. A longer resume delay is visually jarring and conflicts with the worker's expectation of instant context restoration.

items.text: The Desklet Client Framework SHALL transition a desklet to quiesced state within 1s of receiving a quiesce command from the host, suspending context subscription and blanking the display.
items.rationale: Quiesce commands are issued during meeting privacy events (e.g., host locks screen). A 1s transition window ensures context data is not visible to bystanders after the lock event. Longer delays create a confidentiality exposure window.

items.text: The Desklet Client Framework SHALL render the assigned desklet role in the Desklet Shell Renderer within 500ms of receiving the role assignment via the WebSocket control channel.
items.rationale: 500ms render latency is the threshold above which knowledge workers perceive a delay as disruptive context-switching (established from UX literature on notification latency). A role assignment failing to render within this window leaves the desklet displaying stale content while the worker is already in the new task context.

items.text: When the host process restarts after an unplanned termination, the Workspace Context Store SHALL rebuild the authoritative in-memory context snapshot from the Device Identity Ledger (SUB-CTX-036) for pairing records and from a fresh host workspace event subscription within 2 s of process restart, and SHALL mark all context attributes as stale until the first successful event from each subscribed integration is processed.
items.rationale: SUB-CTX-030 holds the authoritative context in memory only; SUB-CTX-036 persists pairing but not context state. Without a defined rebuild sequence, a host process crash leaves every paired desklet unable to receive context updates until an operator intervenes. The 2-second rebuild window is derived from SUB-CTX-032's 30 ms per-event translation latency and the typical warm-reconnect time for host workspace event subscriptions; stale marking prevents desklets from acting on pre-crash context that may no longer reflect workspace reality.

items.text: The Event Bus Adapter SHALL enforce a bounded event ingestion queue of at most 200 pending context-update operations; when the queue reaches capacity, the adapter SHALL apply a drop-oldest eviction policy, increment an overflow counter, and emit a ContextOverflow notification on the event bus within 10 ms of the first dropped event.
items.rationale: IFC-CTX-033 exposes a synchronous 50 ms write API with no queue-depth or back-pressure specification, leaving the Context Object Registry vulnerable to event storms. A 200-event bounded queue with drop-oldest ensures the registry always holds the most recent state; the overflow notification allows upstream components to detect ingestion saturation without polling.

items.text: While in Degraded mode (one or more host integrations unavailable), the Workspace Context Store SHALL continue serving the last successfully applied context snapshot to each paired desklet at the full streaming rate (>= 10 Hz), and SHALL mark context attributes sourced from unavailable integrations as stale in the projection, while maintaining all other attributes as current.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-013 requires last-known context rendering in Degraded mode. Marking stale attributes prevents the desklet from acting on outdated data (e.g. an old Actions list when the Actions integration is offline) while allowing Status and Project views to continue displaying current data where available. The full streaming rate requirement ensures Degraded mode does not change the desklet's update cadence, which would require separate desklet-side handling.

items.text: When the host enters the Locked state, the Workspace Context Store SHALL cease delivering role-scoped projections to the Local Transport Server within 1 s of receiving the lock transition signal, and SHALL resume delivery within 2 s of receiving the unlock signal following successful operator authentication.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-003 requires context streaming to cease within 1 s of host lock. Separating the streaming gate from the Context Object Registry (which continues to accept writes during Locked) allows the host context model to remain current so re-streaming after unlock is immediate rather than requiring a resync. The 2 s resume window is the tail budget after SLM's 1 s authentication path.

items.text: The Event Bus Adapter SHALL subscribe to host workspace events (tool open/close, window focus change, project switch, command execution outcome) and SHALL translate each event into a Context Object Registry write operation within 30 ms of event receipt, emitting a ContextUpdated notification on each successful write.
items.rationale: The Event Bus Adapter is the sole ingestion path into the Context Object Registry per the ARC-REQ-012 design decision. The 30 ms translation latency is budgeted from the total 50 ms write window (SUB-CTX-030) to leave processing headroom. Subscribing to command execution outcomes (from Workspace Executor and Remote Action Gateway) ensures context reflects the results of desklet-initiated actions without the subsystems coupling directly to the store.

items.text: The Role Scope Filter SHALL produce a role-scoped context projection for each paired desklet by applying a deterministic filter mask corresponding to the desklet's assigned role ({Project, Actions, Status, Capture, Logs, AI}), excluding context attributes not defined for that role, and SHALL deliver the projection within 100 ms of a ContextUpdated event.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-011 requires per-role scoping; SYS-REQ-007 defines the canonical desklet role set as {Project, Actions, Status, Capture, Logs, AI} and SUB-PAIR-037 enforces binding to exactly this set. The 100 ms budget is derived from the 200 ms end-to-end streaming latency target (SYS-REQ-006) minus 50 ms write latency (SUB-CTX-030) minus 50 ms transport overhead, ensuring desklet views reflect host state within the user-perceptible threshold for a responsive surface.

items.text: The Context Object Registry SHALL maintain in memory a single authoritative snapshot of the active workspace context, including open tool identifiers, window layout state, active project reference, last 50 command history entries, and per-tool status indicators, and SHALL apply write operations within 50 ms of receipt.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-012 requires a single authoritative context model. The 50 ms write latency ensures the downstream streaming path (Role Scope Filter → Local Transport Server) reflects changes before the next heartbeat cycle, keeping desklet views current. History depth of 50 entries covers a typical working session without unbounded memory growth.

items.text: The External Application Boundary SHALL scope BASIC adapter local script execution to a declared working directory specified in the adapter manifest and SHALL restrict keyboard simulation to the foreground application window at the time of invocation; the adapter SHALL NOT be able to execute scripts outside its declared scope path or simulate keystrokes to windows other than the current foreground window.
items.rationale: IFC-REQ-002 gates BASIC adapter actions through the allowlist but provides no sandboxing of execution scope or keystroke target. Without path scoping, a registered adapter performing local script execution can traverse to arbitrary filesystem locations. Without foreground-window restriction, keyboard simulation can synthesise keystrokes into privileged system dialogs or other applications. These constraints reduce the blast radius of a compromised or misconfigured BASIC adapter to its declared operational scope.

items.text: The External Application Boundary SHALL authenticate each BASIC adapter at registration by verifying a cryptographic manifest signature against the adapter developer's public key stored in the host trust store, and SHALL reject registration of any adapter whose manifest signature is absent, invalid, or signed by an untrusted key.
items.rationale: STK-REQ-008 bounds adapter capabilities at registration time but does not specify how adapter trust is established. Without manifest signature verification, a malicious or tampered BASIC adapter can register with arbitrary capability declarations and gain access to privileged actions (local script execution, keyboard simulation) with no authentication barrier. Cryptographic signing ties the registered capability set to an auditable developer identity and makes tampering detectable at registration rather than at runtime.

items.text: The Adapter Registry SHALL provide fault tolerance against process crashes by persisting its registration state to a local durable store (append-only log or equivalent) after each registration or deregistration event; on process restart, all previously registered adapters SHALL be restored within 3 s (failover recovery) without requiring re-registration by external callers.
items.rationale: The Adapter Registry (UHT: System-Essential, hex 40B53100) is the authoritative index of available adapters; in-memory-only state means a process restart would leave every registered adapter invisible, requiring out-of-band re-registration and creating a silent availability gap. Persist-then-recover restores the registry atomically on restart, preserving the SYS-REQ-014 constraint that only registered adapters may participate in action brokering.

items.text: When the Action Broker terminates unexpectedly, the Process Supervisor SHALL provide fault recovery by detecting the failure within 2 s and restarting the Action Broker within 5 s; the fault-tolerant fallback SHALL queue intent dispatches received during the restart interval and resubmit them automatically once the Action Broker confirms readiness, with no intent silently dropped.
items.rationale: The Action Broker (UHT: System-Essential, hex 41B57900) is the gatekeeper for all adapter-mediated actions; an unrecovered crash would stall all pending intents with no error feedback to the desklet. Supervisor restart within 5 s (two heartbeat cycles) keeps the system within the SYS-REQ-009 500 ms intent-reflection window for the common case, and queue-then-resubmit ensures the no-silent-drop property required for command-idempotency guarantees.

items.text: The Adapter Manifest Loader SHALL implement fault-tolerant manifest loading: it SHALL cache the last successfully validated adapter manifest in memory and, when the manifest file becomes unreadable or unavailable at runtime, SHALL continue serving the cached manifest (failover to last-good state) and emit a WARN log entry; the Adapter Framework SHALL admit no new adapters until a valid manifest is reloaded.
items.rationale: The Adapter Manifest Loader (UHT: System-Essential, hex 41B53B00) is the sole source of adapter schema definitions; a crash or file-system error would otherwise clear the registry, halting all adapter-mediated actions. Caching the last-good manifest limits the blast radius of a manifest file fault to 'no new adapters' rather than 'all adapters lost', satisfying the SYS-REQ-016 availability requirement.

items.text: The Adapter Manifest Loader SHALL validate each manifest file against the ContextRail adapter manifest schema before loading; manifests that fail schema validation, reference non-existent executables, or set permission flags outside the declared adapter scope SHALL be rejected with a structured error log entry and SHALL NOT be loaded into the Adapter Registry.
items.rationale: Invalid or malformed manifests that load silently could result in runtime errors during intent execution or security boundary violations. Schema validation at load time converts a runtime failure mode into a startup or hot-reload failure mode where it can be surfaced to the host operator before any intents are dispatched.

items.text: The Adapter Registry SHALL reject any adapter registration where the declared executable path lies outside the host-configured adapter-approved directory tree, and SHALL refuse to serve lookup results for rejected or deregistered adapters.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-001 requires all sensitive actions to execute on the host within controlled paths. Unrestricted executable paths in adapters would allow arbitrary command injection by a malicious adapter manifest file; directory-tree scoping enforces a capability boundary analogous to a chroot for adapter scripts.

items.text: The Action Broker SHALL NOT forward an AdaptedCommand to the Workspace Executor until it has received an explicit PERMIT response from the Policy Engine for the adapter ID and action class; a DENY response or Policy Engine non-response within 10 ms SHALL result in the intent being rejected with a DENY outcome and a security violation log entry.
items.rationale: IFC-ADP-023 defines the permit check; the Action Broker is the enforcement point for adapter-scoped allowlist policy. Without this gate an external application could invoke any command the adapter has access to, bypassing the Knowledge Worker action governance model. The 10ms non-response DENY aligns with the Policy Engine SLA in IFC-ADP-023.

items.text: The Allowlist and Configuration Governance subsystem SHALL implement allowlist access control in conformance with IEC 62443 (Security for industrial automation and control systems) Part 3-3 SR 2.1 (Authorisation enforcement), such that every action evaluated against the allowlist is subject to a defined policy and all allowlist change events are attributed to an authenticated operator session.
items.rationale: The Allowlist Gate (UHT: Institutionally Defined, hex 40A53950) enforces the boundary between permitted and prohibited actions; IEC 62443-3-3 SR 2.1 (Authorisation Enforcement) is the applicable standard for allowlist-based access control in a system integrating external tools (adapters) into a privileged execution path. Attribution of every allowlist change to an authenticated HAS session satisfies the accountability requirement of that control and supports audit investigation if the allowlist is manipulated.

items.text: The Allowlist Store SHALL persist allowlist entries atomically to the host filesystem such that a process restart or power cycle does not result in partial writes, and the stored entries SHALL be identical to the last successfully committed state within 100 ms of a write acknowledgement.
items.rationale: Allowlist integrity across restarts is essential — a partial write could leave the Policy Engine with an inconsistent allow set, permitting previously denied actions or denying previously allowed ones. Atomic writes (e.g., write-then-rename) prevent torn states. The 100 ms window bounds the maximum allowlist staleness visible to the Policy Engine after a crash-recovery.

items.text: The Maintenance Configuration Interface SHALL reject all add, remove, and list allowlist operations with an explicit error when ContextRail is not in Maintenance mode, and SHALL permit such operations only when the host process is running in Maintenance mode as signalled by the Host Core Runtime.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-005 requires that allowlist management is accessible only via the host and only in Maintenance mode. Gating the interface on mode state prevents an operator error or a compromised context from modifying the allowlist during normal operation, when the Policy Engine is actively enforcing decisions from it.

items.text: The Policy Engine SHALL, upon issuing a DENY decision, emit a structured log entry to the Allowlist Audit Logger containing the action identifier, principal subsystem identity, timestamp (UTC, ms precision), and reason code before returning the DENY response to the caller.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-004 requires that denied actions are logged — the log entry is the evidential record that the gate functioned. Emitting the log entry before returning the response ensures the audit trail is complete even if the caller does not record the rejection.

items.text: The Policy Engine SHALL evaluate every action request from the Adapter Framework or Remote Action Gateway against the Allowlist Store using default-deny semantics and SHALL return an explicit ALLOW or DENY decision within 5 ms at the 99th percentile under nominal load.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-004 requires evaluation of every host-mediated external and remote action against the active allowlist. The 5 ms gate latency is allocated from the 200 ms end-to-end intent round-trip (SYS-REQ-009): gate evaluation is expected to consume <3% of the total budget. Default-deny ensures any unlisted action is rejected without requiring exhaustive enumeration of disallowed actions.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL operate over a host-exposed local web server using WebSocket Secure (WSS) messaging on the local network, assuming no inbound internet exposure to the host.
items.rationale: Operating environment, network constraint: the transport assumption (LAN-only, no inbound internet) bounds the threat surface and pairing trust model. Specifying WSS rather than plain WebSocket ensures that even on a LAN, eavesdropping by co-resident processes or devices is prevented; this is consistent with the TLS 1.3 requirement in IFC-XPT-019 and the role-scoped context confidentiality requirement in STK-REQ-004.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL remain fully functional with no required cloud service and SHALL depend on no external internet connectivity for core operation.
items.rationale: Operating environment (INV-01): local-first operation is a defining constraint; any cloud dependency would reintroduce the third-party execution exposure ContextRail is designed to avoid.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL execute remote actions using only the Workstation Owner's existing SSH configuration, so that the Remote Service Owner's established access controls remain authoritative.
items.rationale: Remote Service Owner, credential-reuse scenario: reusing existing SSH config means no new standing credential or trust path is created on the service owner's estate.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL restrict host-mediated SSH actions against Remote Service Owner infrastructure to an explicitly allowlisted command set (service status, log tailing, restart, deploy, backup, health checks), and SHALL NOT expose arbitrary SSH.
items.rationale: Remote Service Owner, remote-action scenario: the service owner relies on the allowlist to guarantee that a compromised or careless desklet path cannot run arbitrary commands on their infrastructure.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL allow the Integration Adapter Developer to register an integration adapter whose capabilities are bounded by the host capability model at registration time.
items.rationale: Integration Adapter Developer, adapter-registration scenario: registration is the point at which an adapter's reach is constrained, so it must be an explicit, bounded operation.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL expose a stable adapter contract with defined capability boundaries, so the Integration Adapter Developer can author BASIC and DEEP adapters without access to host credentials.
items.rationale: Integration Adapter Developer, adapter-authoring scenario (INV-04): a stable, credential-free contract is what lets third-party adapters be written safely and survive host upgrades.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL allow the Workstation Owner to place the host into a Locked confidentiality safe state in which desklets stream no context and execute no intents until the operator re-authenticates at the host.
items.rationale: Workstation Owner, Locked-mode scenario: when the workstation is unattended, confidentiality of streamed context must collapse to a defined safe state under owner control.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL allow the Workstation Owner to govern the set of permitted host-mediated remote and external actions through an explicit, owner-editable allowlist.
items.rationale: Workstation Owner, allowlist-governance scenario (OQ-04): the owner must be able to bound what desklets can ultimately cause the host to do, or the execution boundary is unenforceable.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL confine all credentials, network access, and sensitive execution to the host, so that the Workstation Owner retains sole control of the confidentiality and execution boundary.
items.rationale: Workstation Owner, security-boundary scenario (INV-02/INV-03): the owner accepts the system only if no secret or execution capability leaves the host onto user-owned spare devices.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL present each Knowledge Worker desklet with a single assigned role drawn from {Project, Actions, Status, Capture, Logs, AI}, rendered from host-held workspace context.
items.rationale: Knowledge Worker, role-rendering scenario: one role per desklet keeps each cheap surface purposeful and avoids crowding every tool onto one screen, which is the problem ContextRail exists to solve.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL allow the Knowledge Worker to dispatch high-level workspace intents from a desklet and have the resulting status reflected interactively on that desklet.
items.rationale: Knowledge Worker, intent-dispatch scenario: the desklet is a command surface, so an intent must produce visible, timely feedback or the worker cannot trust the distributed surface.

items.text: The ContextRail SHALL allow the Knowledge Worker to repurpose a spare browser/PWA device as a role-bound desklet by scanning a QR code or opening a local URL, without installing any native application.
items.rationale: Knowledge Worker, device-pairing scenario: the value proposition is extending the control surface across idle devices with zero native-app friction; a native install requirement would defeat the spare-device premise.

items.text: The interface between the Channel Multiplexer and the Intent Router SHALL forward each inbound intent message received from a desklet WebSocket connection as a JSON envelope containing: desklet ID, role, intent type, payload, and receipt timestamp, delivered synchronously over an in-process call within 2 ms.
items.rationale: The Intent Router requires the desklet ID and role to apply allowlist filtering (SYS-REQ-004) and to serialise conflicting intents (SYS-REQ-010). Synchronous in-process delivery within 2 ms meets the interactive response budget without introducing queuing jitter.

items.text: The interface between the Channel Multiplexer and the Workspace Context Store SHALL accept context delta frames encoded as JSON messages with a role-scope field, a sequence number, and a delta payload, delivered over an in-process EventEmitter or equivalent; the Channel Multiplexer SHALL acknowledge each frame within 5 ms.
items.rationale: In-process EventEmitter eliminates serialisation overhead on the hot context-streaming path. The role-scope field is the minimum metadata needed for fan-out routing; the sequence number enables the desklet client to detect dropped frames and request a full snapshot refresh.

items.text: The interface between the Heartbeat Monitor and the Connection Registry SHALL allow the Heartbeat Monitor to read the full connection list and last-pong timestamps within 2 ms and to mark a connection as DEAD within 5 ms; both operations SHALL be atomic with respect to concurrent writes from the WebSocket Gateway.
items.rationale: Atomicity is required because the WebSocket Gateway may concurrently write a reconnect event while the Heartbeat Monitor is expiring the old connection. Without atomic reads/writes, a reconnect could be lost, causing an immediate false disconnect cascade.

items.text: The interface between the WebSocket Gateway and the Connection Registry SHALL update the registry within 10 ms of any socket connect or disconnect event, providing at minimum: desklet ID, socket handle reference, assigned role, and ISO 8601 event timestamp.
items.rationale: The Connection Registry is the single source of truth for routing decisions made by the Channel Multiplexer and liveness decisions made by the Heartbeat Monitor. A 10 ms write latency bound ensures these consumers see current state before their next decision cycle (2 s heartbeat interval).

items.text: The interface between the HTTP Static Asset Server and the WebSocket Gateway SHALL route all HTTP requests with Connection: Upgrade and Upgrade: websocket headers to the WebSocket Gateway and SHALL serve all other GET requests as static file responses from the pre-built asset bundle.
items.rationale: A single-port upgrade routing pattern eliminates the need for CORS configuration and simplifies the desklet's bootstrap: one URL serves both the initial page load and the subsequent WebSocket connection, making port management trivial for the end user.

items.text: The interface between the Local Transport Server and the Host Core Runtime SHALL use the subsystem heartbeat protocol (HTTP GET /health at 2 s intervals on the loopback interface, port configurable via the Configuration Loader); the Local Transport Server SHALL respond within 200 ms with JSON payload containing: status (nominal|degraded|unavailable), active session count, and peak RTT over the last 10 s; a failure to respond within 200 ms SHALL be treated by the Health Watchdog as a missed heartbeat per SUB-HCR-018.
items.rationale: HCR's Health Watchdog monitors all registered subsystems via the heartbeat protocol (SUB-HCR-018). XPT must implement this interface so the Mode State Machine can demote the system to Degraded mode when transport connectivity fails, satisfying the desklet isolation requirement that follows from the 'isolate failed desklet path' function.

items.text: The interface between the Local Transport Server and the Desklet Client Framework SHALL use WSS (WebSocket Secure) over TLS 1.3 with a server-issued session token in the initial HTTP Upgrade handshake; the server SHALL reject upgrade requests bearing a token that has expired (token TTL: 60 s from issuance by the Desklet Pairing and Identity subsystem) and SHALL send a 401 Unauthorised close frame with a machine-readable error code.
items.rationale: The 'serve desklet web surface' and 'stream role-scoped context to desklets' functions both traverse the XPT–DCF boundary over a persistent channel. The TLS 1.3 + short-lived token requirement is derived from the Host Authenticator authentication standard (SLM) and the desklet-pairing identity model; unauthenticated channels would bypass the full confidentiality protection established in IFC-SLM-005.

items.text: The interface between the Lock State Controller and the Intent Router and Local Transport Server SHALL deliver a Locked or Operational state change notification synchronously within the same process event loop tick, ensuring no intent or context message is processed between the LSC state transition and the downstream suspension.
items.rationale: Synchronous intra-process notification prevents a race where the intent router accepts an intent in the gap between the LSC state change and the router's local awareness of it. Asynchronous delivery (e.g. via message queue) would create an unavoidable race window incompatible with the 1s quiesce requirement.

items.text: The interface between the Host Authenticator and the OS session manager SHALL use the OS-native lock event subscription mechanism (e.g. systemd-logind DBus signals on Linux) and SHALL not require polling; the Host Authenticator SHALL receive the event within 100 ms of the OS generating it.
items.rationale: Push subscription rather than polling eliminates the lock-detection jitter that would otherwise accumulate; 100ms is the budget for this interface hop derived from the 200ms HAUTH→LSC signalling requirement (SUB-SLM-003) minus 100ms for in-process processing.

items.text: The interface between the Pairing Token Authority and the Local Transport Server SHALL exchange pairing token challenges and responses over an in-process function call, completing validation within 20 ms under normal host load.
items.rationale: In-process call rather than IPC ensures token material never crosses a process boundary; 20ms budget is allocated from the overall 3s join completion window in SYS-REQ-006, leaving margin for WebSocket handshake and role assignment.

items.text: The interface between ContextRail and Remote Service Owner infrastructure (Remote SSH Boundary) SHALL use SSH restricted to the allowlisted command set (service status, log tailing, restart, deploy, backup, health checks) and SHALL use the host operator's existing SSH configuration.
items.rationale: External interface RSB owned by Remote Service Owner: the allowlisted command set and reused SSH config are the contract that bounds host-mediated remote action.

items.text: The interface between the Remote Action Gateway and the Remote SSH Boundary SHALL accept SSH action requests as structured JSON objects containing command text, target host alias, adapter identity, and command class (bounded|streaming); for bounded-class commands the interface SHALL return a JSON result containing exit code, stdout, stderr, and execution duration within 32 s; for streaming-class commands (deploy, backup) the interface SHALL return an initiating acknowledgement within 2 s and SHALL stream incremental JSON result fragments until the remote process exits or is cancelled.
items.rationale: The two-path interface reflects the command-class split in SUB-RSB-061: bounded commands (status, log tail, restart, health check) complete within 32 s (30 s execution + 2 s overhead); streaming commands (deploy, backup) have no bounded duration and require a streaming response pattern consistent with IFC-RAG-026. Separating command class in the request allows the policy engine to enforce appropriate timeout enforcement per class.

items.text: The interface between the SSH Session Manager and the Remote Action Result Emitter SHALL transfer the raw command result as an in-process struct containing: exit_code (int), stdout (string, max 1 MiB), stderr (string, max 64 KiB), duration_ms (int), and a connection_error field (nullable string); transfer SHALL complete within 10 ms of session close.
items.rationale: Size limits on stdout/stderr prevent memory exhaustion from commands producing unbounded output. The nullable connection_error field cleanly separates SSH layer failures from application-level exit codes, enabling the Result Emitter to produce typed error envelopes.

items.text: The interface between the Allowlist Gate and the SSH Session Manager SHALL pass a structured permit token containing: target host, port, command pattern, and the matching allowlist rule ID; the SSH Session Manager SHALL bind each session exclusively to the permit token's target host and port.
items.rationale: The permit token binds the allowed host/port/command at gate-time so the SSH Session Manager cannot execute on a different target than what the allowlist evaluated. Without this coupling, a TOCTOU attack could replace the target after the permit decision.

items.text: The interface between the Remote Action Gateway and the Workspace Executor SHALL use the CommandEnvelope channel defined in IFC-EXE-017 with the adapter ID field set to 'rag' and the environment overrides field carrying the SSH_SESSION_ID and REMOTE_USER values from the originating SSH session; the Remote Action Gateway SHALL forward the command only after receiving a PERMIT from the Policy Engine (IFC-RAG-025); the CommandResult SHALL be streamed back to the SSH channel as it is produced, not buffered until process exit.
items.rationale: The 'broker remote SSH action' function requires RAG to translate SSH commands into CommandEnvelopes for Workspace Executor. Streaming the result without buffering is required because remote SSH sessions commonly drive long-running build or test commands where output latency matters; buffering until exit would create unacceptable lag for interactive remote operator sessions.

items.text: The interface between the Remote Action Gateway and the Allowlist and Configuration Governance Policy Engine SHALL use the synchronous ActionPermitRequest RPC defined in IFC-ADP-023; the Remote Action Gateway SHALL present: gateway ID, remote action class (shell-command|script|query), target resource URI, and the originating SSH session identifier; the Policy Engine SHALL respond within 10 ms; a DENY SHALL cause the Remote Action Gateway to terminate the SSH channel with exit code 126 and a structured error payload.
items.rationale: Remote SSH actions carry a higher risk profile than local application actions because the source is an authenticated external operator. The same synchronous PERMIT gate used by the Adapter Framework (IFC-ADP-023) is applied to RAG to ensure uniform allowlist enforcement. The 126 exit code and structured payload are chosen to be distinguishable by automated CI/CD callers from a subprocess error (exit 1) or authentication failure (exit 255).

items.text: The interface between the Heartbeat Monitor and the Device Identity Ledger SHALL provide a read-only query that returns the list of currently registered device identifiers and their last-seen timestamps without acquiring any write lock on the ledger, and SHALL return consistent results even if a pairing or de-registration write is in progress concurrently.
items.rationale: The Heartbeat Monitor polls the DIL at 1 Hz; if the read path blocks on writes, a slow pairing operation can delay heartbeat probes, causing false link-lost declarations. Read-stable access (e.g. copy-on-write or snapshot reads) ensures liveness monitoring is independent of pairing throughput.

items.text: The interface between the Pairing Token Generator and the Device Identity Ledger SHALL use a synchronous registration call that accepts a device identifier and returns a host-assigned registration record, SHALL complete within 50 ms, and SHALL be atomic — the desklet is either fully registered or not registered at all, with no partial-registration state observable by callers.
items.rationale: Atomicity prevents a partial registration from appearing in the Heartbeat Monitor's device list, which could trigger spurious heartbeat probes to an identity not yet bound to a role. The 50 ms budget aligns with the overall pairing handshake latency target implied by SYS-REQ-002 (join triggers immediate authentication).

items.text: The interface between the Desklet Pairing and Identity subsystem and the Security and Lock Manager Pairing Token Authority SHALL use an in-process TokenValidationRequest (token bytes, device fingerprint, timestamp) → TokenValidationResponse (valid|invalid|expired, bound role, expiry epoch); the Desklet Pairing and Identity subsystem SHALL reject any join request where the response is invalid or expired, SHALL NOT retry on the same token, and SHALL emit a PairingRejected audit event within 100 ms of the rejection decision.
items.rationale: The 'monitor desklet liveness' and 're-bind desklet role on reconnect' functions both depend on Desklet Pairing and Identity holding a verified role binding. The role binding is originally established by the Pairing Token Authority (SUB-SLM-001 and SUB-SLM-002); this interface requirement ensures that the PAIR subsystem verifies the token before extending any session, preventing a token-reuse attack where a stolen or expired OTT is replayed against a new connection.

items.text: The interface between the Desklet Pairing and Identity subsystem and the Local Transport Server SHALL use an HTTP POST /pair endpoint on the loopback interface; the pairing request SHALL carry the one-time token (OTT) from the QR code, the desklet device fingerprint, and the requested role; the Desklet Pairing and Identity subsystem SHALL verify the OTT within 2 s and respond with a signed session token (60 s TTL); the Local Transport Server SHALL issue a WSS session only to callers presenting a valid signed session token from this endpoint.
items.rationale: The 'pair desklet via QR code' function requires a secure one-time-token exchange before the Local Transport Server grants a persistent WSS session. The 2 s verification deadline prevents brute-force timing attacks on OTT validation. The session token TTL of 60 s is consistent with IFC-XPT-019 and the Pairing Token Authority issuance policy (SUB-SLM-001).

items.text: The interface between the Knowledge Worker Desklet Station and the Desklet Web Boundary SHALL present an initial pairing screen requesting QR scan on first connection, and SHALL suppress pairing re-scan for already-paired sessions that reconnect within the same session lifetime.
items.rationale: Pairing on first connection enforces SYS-REQ-002 (single-use token binding); suppressing re-pairing on reconnect implements SYS-REQ-008 (restore prior role binding on reconnect) without requiring the operator to re-scan, which would be disruptive to a Knowledge Worker during brief link interruptions.

items.text: The interface between the Intent Dispatcher and the Dispatch Confirmer SHALL carry a dispatch-tracking record containing the intent request ID, the target executor endpoint, and the latency deadline timestamp, allowing the Dispatch Confirmer to track the execution outcome and emit a TIMEOUT event independently if no acknowledgment is received within the latency budget.
items.rationale: Passing the latency deadline timestamp at the dispatch-tracking interface boundary allows the Dispatch Confirmer to enforce SYS-REQ-009 autonomously, ensuring TIMEOUT is emitted even if the downstream executor hangs and never sends an acknowledgment.

items.text: The interface between the Conflict Serialiser and the Intent Dispatcher for ordered intent release SHALL deliver the head-of-queue intent to the Intent Dispatcher within 1 ms of the preceding intent completing execution, ensuring forward progress of the per-context-object serialisation queue with no starvation under continuous concurrent intent load.
items.rationale: Bounded release latency from CSER to IDISP is required to prevent serialisation queue starvation when multiple desklets continuously target the same context object; the 1 ms bound keeps the ordering mechanism's own overhead negligible relative to the 200 ms end-to-end budget.

items.text: The interface between the Intent Dispatcher and the Conflict Serialiser SHALL accept a synchronous conflict-check call carrying the intent ID and target context-object reference, and SHALL return either a queue slot or a SUPERSEDED signal within 2 ms at the 99th percentile under nominal system load.
items.rationale: The IDISP→CSER interface is on the critical 200 ms end-to-end path; a 2 ms bound at P99 preserves sufficient latency headroom for ACG gate check, conflict resolution, downstream dispatch, and confirmation delivery within the budget established by SYS-REQ-009.

items.text: The interface between the Configuration Loader and the Startup Orchestrator SHALL provide the validated configuration as an in-memory, read-only data structure before the Startup Orchestrator starts any subsystem; the Configuration Loader SHALL signal READY only after schema validation passes, and the Startup Orchestrator SHALL treat absence of the READY signal within 5 s as a BOOT_FAILED condition.
items.rationale: Configuration must be available before any subsystem starts to prevent subsystems from operating with undefined adapter registrations or transport addresses; the 5s timeout aligns with the per-subsystem startup deadline in SUB-HCR-019.

items.text: The interface between the Startup Orchestrator and the Mode State Machine SHALL deliver a BOOT_COMPLETE event carrying a list of all successfully started subsystems within 30 s of process start; the Mode State Machine SHALL use this event as the sole trigger to transition from a pre-boot state into Nominal mode.
items.rationale: The 30s total boot deadline is derived from SYS-REQ-006's 3s desklet admission window — the system must be fully booted before any desklet can successfully join; the single-event trigger prevents the Mode State Machine from entering Nominal mode before all subsystems are confirmed ready.

items.text: The interface between the Health Watchdog and the Mode State Machine SHALL use an asynchronous event channel; the Health Watchdog SHALL emit SUBSYSTEM_FAILED and SUBSYSTEM_RECOVERED events with the subsystem identity and timestamp, and the Mode State Machine SHALL process each event within 100 ms of receipt.
items.rationale: The event channel must be asynchronous to prevent a slow or hung subsystem health check from blocking the Mode State Machine's ability to process recovery events from other subsystems; the 100ms processing budget preserves the 500ms total broadcast budget from SUB-HCR-017.

items.text: The interface between the Host Administration Station and the Allowlist and Configuration Governance subsystem SHALL accept allowlist entries as structured objects (adapter name, action pattern, allow/deny) and SHALL confirm application of the change within 2 s or return a rejection reason.
items.rationale: 2-second confirmation window provides operator feedback within a comfortable CLI interaction timeframe; structured input prevents shell injection through improperly escaped allowlist entries.

items.text: The interface between the Process Supervisor and the Outcome Reporter SHALL pass a ProcessResult struct containing exit code, status (SUCCESS, FAILURE, TIMEOUT), stdout digest, truncation flag, elapsed milliseconds, and intent ID; the Outcome Reporter SHALL forward all fields unmodified when constructing the CommandOutcome event.
items.rationale: The CommandOutcome event fields are derived directly from the ProcessResult; if any field is dropped or transformed at this boundary the Workspace Context Store receives an incomplete event that cannot be correlated or used for audit. Requiring field-complete forwarding makes the Outcome Reporter a transparent formatter rather than a business-logic component, simplifying testing.

items.text: The interface between the Command Dispatcher and the Process Supervisor SHALL pass a typed execution request struct containing the validated target process path, argument vector, resolved environment map, and intent ID; the Process Supervisor SHALL not accept any execution request that omits the intent ID field.
items.rationale: The intent ID must propagate from CommandEnvelope receipt through subprocess invocation to the Outcome Reporter to satisfy the IFC-EXE-017 audit chain requirement. Rejecting structs without intent ID at the interface boundary prevents silent ID loss inside the Executor which would break outcome-to-intent correlation in the Workspace Context Store.

items.text: The interface between the Workspace Executor and the Workspace Context Store SHALL transmit a CommandOutcome event (status, exit code, stdout digest, elapsed ms, intent ID) over the local event bus within 50 ms of command completion; the Workspace Context Store SHALL reject any outcome event whose intent ID does not correspond to a dispatched intent in its active-intent index.
items.rationale: The 'report action outcome' function (FBS) requires Workspace Executor to close the context-update loop by publishing structured outcomes to Workspace Context Store. Without a typed event contract with intent-ID binding the Context Store cannot correlate outcomes to the correct workspace context object, producing stale or inconsistent state visible to Knowledge Worker desklets.

items.text: The interface between the Workspace Executor and the Intent Router SHALL use a typed in-process command envelope (CommandEnvelope) containing: action identifier, target process path, argument vector, environment overrides, and a monotonic intent ID; the Executor SHALL return a CommandResult within 200 ms of process exit or yield a timeout fault if the subprocess exceeds the SYS-REQ-009 200 ms execution budget.
items.rationale: The Intent Execution Mediation capability (FBS) places Workspace Executor on the critical latency path from intent dispatch to action outcome. The CommandEnvelope contract is needed to decouple the Intent Router's dispatch logic from execution details while preserving intent identity through the execution chain for audit and context-update correlation.

items.text: The interface between ContextRail and external applications and OS tools (External Application Boundary) SHALL be mediated by BASIC adapters supporting application launch, URL open, local script execution, keyboard simulation, and workspace restore, each invocation passing through the allowlist gate.
items.rationale: External interface EAB owned by third-party/OS: BASIC-adapter mediation plus the gate is what keeps API-optional control inside the execution boundary.

items.text: The interface between the External Application Boundary and the Adapter Framework SHALL accept action dispatch requests and return a normalised result within 6 s, where a 5 s callback timeout plus 1 s for serialisation, network, and validation constitutes the full request budget.
items.rationale: 6-second total budget encompasses the 5-second HTTP callback timeout (the primary latency contributor) plus up to 1 second for JSON serialisation, local network round-trip, and response validation; exceeding this budget causes the Adapter Framework to time-out the action and report failure to the Intent Router.

items.text: The interface between ContextRail and the spare-device browser/PWA runtime (Desklet Web Boundary) SHALL use WebSocket Secure (WSS) over TLS 1.3 on the local network and SHALL carry only role-scoped context and high-level intent messages, never credentials or executable payloads.
items.rationale: STK-REQ-004 makes role-scoped context confidentiality the core system premise; plaintext WebSocket on the LAN would expose context to any other device or process on the network. IFC-XPT-019 mandates TLS 1.3 with a session token for the XPT-DCF channel; IFC-CTX-022 carries SHA-256 integrity fields over the WSS channel. Aligning IFC-REQ-001 with these detailed transport requirements gives an implementer a consistent, unambiguous TLS posture at every layer.

items.text: The interface between the Desklet Web Boundary and the Intent Router SHALL accept validated intent frames as JSON objects with type, payload, correlation ID, and desklet identity, and SHALL return a dispatch acknowledgement within 100 ms.
items.rationale: 100 ms acknowledgement window bounds the latency perceived by the desklet user between intent submission and feedback; exceeding it causes the desklet UI to show a stale 'pending' indicator. Validated frame format prevents the Intent Router from parsing raw WebSocket payloads.

items.text: The interface between ContextRail and DEEP-tier integrations (Deep Integration Boundary) SHALL exchange structured workspace context via MCP servers and browser/IDE extension APIs, confined to each adapter's registered capability set.
items.rationale: External interface DIB owned by third-party/developer: structured context exchange is the richer integration tier and must remain capability-bounded per INV-04.

items.text: The interface between the Deep Integration Boundary and the Allowlist and Configuration Governance subsystem SHALL receive updated capability scope policies within 2 s of an allowlist change event, using a push notification over the internal policy bus.
items.rationale: 2-second policy propagation window is the same bound applied to the SUB-DIB scope enforcement requirement; a push model is required (rather than polling) to meet the window reliably without adding unnecessary CPU load from frequent polling cycles.

items.text: The interface between the Deep Integration Boundary and the Workspace Context Store SHALL receive workspace context change events via the internal event bus and SHALL deliver events to subscribed DEEP adapters within 50 ms of publication.
items.rationale: The 50 ms delivery target propagates the SYS-REQ-012 context propagation requirement through the deep adapter path; failure to meet this creates observable lag between desklet context and adapter-side state, breaking co-located automation scenarios.

items.text: The interface between the Deep Integration Boundary and the Adapter Framework SHALL exchange adapter action results via synchronous JSON-RPC response within 200 ms of action dispatch, using the DEEP adapter session channel.
items.rationale: 200 ms response window is the action-roundtrip budget allocated to the deep integration path; exceeding it causes the Intent Router to mark the action as timed-out and routes the failure to the originating desklet.

items.text: The interface between the Connection Health Monitor and the Lifecycle State Machine SHALL deliver disconnection and reconnection events via the in-process event bus within 200ms of detection, carrying the failure reason (timeout, protocol error, or network unreachable).
items.rationale: Connection events drive the Lifecycle State Machine into reconnecting or degraded states. The 200ms delivery window is negligible against the 10s detection window; including the failure reason allows the LSM to select the appropriate reconnect strategy.

items.text: The interface between the Lifecycle State Machine and the Desklet Shell Renderer SHALL deliver state transition commands (active, quiesced, resuming) via the in-process event bus within 100ms, triggering the renderer to blank or restore the display accordingly.
items.rationale: State commands are infrequent (human-driven) but time-critical (quiesce must blank display within 1s total budget). The 100ms in-process delivery leaves adequate margin for the render cycle. Commands must be synchronously acknowledged to confirm state change has been applied.

items.text: The interface between the Context Feed Client and the Desklet Shell Renderer SHALL be an in-process event bus delivering context update events within 50ms of WebSocket receipt, including the full current context snapshot in each event payload.
items.rationale: An in-process event bus removes serialisation cost of inter-process communication. Including the full snapshot (not a diff) prevents the renderer needing to maintain incremental state and avoids rendering inconsistencies if an update is missed.

items.text: The interface between the Context Feed Client and the Local Transport Server SHALL use WebSocket (RFC 6455) with JSON-encoded context payloads, supporting a sustained throughput of 10 messages per second per desklet without message loss.
items.rationale: WebSocket is the protocol already used by the Local Transport Server for desklet delivery; aligning the context subscription to the same transport eliminates protocol conversion overhead. 10 msg/s sustained is the Workspace Context Store's maximum publication rate, defining the dimensioning point for the interface.

items.text: The interface between the Role Scope Filter and the Local Transport Server SHALL deliver role-scoped context projections as JSON-serialised messages over an internal push channel, SHALL identify the target desklet by its pairing identity, SHALL support concurrent delivery to up to 8 paired desklets, and SHALL not block delivery to one desklet on an unresponsive desklet connection (one that fails to consume a frame within 500 ms).
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-006 requires the host to serve the desklet web surface over WebSocket; the Local Transport Server owns that transport. Per-desklet non-blocking delivery prevents a single stalled connection (e.g. a desklet on a congested Wi-Fi path) from delaying context updates to all other paired desklets. The 8-desklet concurrency ceiling matches the maximum pairing count implied by the workstation-desk deployment scenario in the ConOps.

items.text: The interface between the Context Object Registry and the Role Scope Filter SHALL expose a read API accepting a role identifier and returning the full workspace context snapshot, SHALL guarantee snapshot consistency (no torn reads across attributes), and SHALL complete read operations within 10 ms.
items.rationale: The Role Scope Filter applies projection masks on top of a full snapshot; a torn read would produce a projection combining attributes from two different workspace states, potentially rendering an inconsistent desklet view (e.g. a stale project name with a new tool list). The 10 ms read budget is sized to fit within the 100 ms projection delivery target (SUB-CTX-031) with margin for filter logic.

items.text: The interface between the Event Bus Adapter and the Context Object Registry SHALL use a typed internal write API accepting context-update operations (attribute path, new value, source-event type, timestamp), SHALL be synchronous from the adapter's perspective with a response within 50 ms, and SHALL reject malformed operations with a typed error without modifying registry state.
items.rationale: The Event Bus Adapter is the sole write path into the Context Object Registry per ARC-REQ-012. A typed write API enforces the contract that all context attributes follow the defined schema, preventing schema drift from host event normalisation. Synchronous response enables the adapter to guarantee ContextUpdated is only emitted on confirmed writes, preserving consistency between the registry and downstream consumers.

items.text: The interface between the Workspace Context Store and the Desklet Client Framework SHALL use server-sent events (SSE) transported over the Local Transport Server WSS channel; the Context Store SHALL send only context attributes whose tag set intersects the desklet's assigned role scope; each SSE payload SHALL be a JSON object with fields: context-object ID, delta fields, version counter, and a SHA-256 digest of the full current attribute set for client-side integrity verification.
items.rationale: The 'render assigned desklet role' and 'stream role-scoped context to desklets' functions require Workspace Context Store to filter outbound context by role membership. Role-scoped filtering is the enforcement point for the Context Access Guard requirement (SUB-SLM-005); without it, a desklet could receive context attributes belonging to a different role, violating workspace confidentiality.

items.text: The interface between the Workspace Context Store and the Intent Router SHALL use an in-process publish-subscribe event bus; the Context Store SHALL publish a ContextUpdated event (context-object ID, delta fields, version counter, source intent ID) within 30 ms of a CommandOutcome receipt; the Intent Router SHALL subscribe to ContextUpdated events and use them to update its per-context routing table within one event-loop cycle after receipt.
items.rationale: The Workspace Context Distribution capability (FBS) requires the Context Store to push context updates back to the Intent Router so that subsequent intents are dispatched against the latest workspace state. A pull model would introduce at least one RTT of staleness on the critical 200 ms intent execution path; the 30 ms publish deadline preserves margin against SYS-REQ-009.
