items.text: The interface between the Adapter Manifest Loader and the Adapter Registry SHALL be a bulk-write in-process call that atomically replaces the registry contents on each hot-reload; during the replacement window the Adapter Registry SHALL continue serving lookups from the previous registry snapshot until the new snapshot is fully committed.
items.rationale: Atomic registry replacement prevents partial-load states where some adapters from a new manifest set are registered and others are not, which would cause inconsistent permit decisions during the reload window. Serving from the previous snapshot during replacement ensures no intent is refused due to a transient empty registry.

items.text: The interface between the Action Broker and the Adapter Registry SHALL be a synchronous in-process lookup by adapter ID returning the resolved executable path and declared permission scope; the lookup SHALL complete within 1 ms; a lookup for an unregistered or deregistered adapter ID SHALL return a NOT_FOUND error and the Action Broker SHALL reject the intent with DENY.
items.rationale: The lookup must be synchronous and sub-millisecond because it is on the intent-execution critical path (SYS-REQ-009 200ms budget). A failed lookup that proceeds to execution would allow intents to target unregistered adapters; returning NOT_FOUND forces a DENY which is the correct safe behaviour.

items.text: The interface between the Adapter Framework and the Workspace Executor SHALL use a typed AdaptedCommand message (adapter ID, resolved command path, argument vector, environment overrides, originating intent ID) delivered via the same CommandEnvelope channel defined in IFC-EXE-017; the Adapter Framework SHALL NOT forward the AdaptedCommand to the Executor until a PERMIT response has been received from the Policy Engine (IFC-ADP-023); a forwarding attempt without a valid PERMIT SHALL be logged as a security violation and the intent SHALL be rejected with a DENY outcome.
items.rationale: The 'broker external application action' function (FBS) requires the Adapter Framework to transform external application intents into Executor-compatible commands. Gating the forwarding on PERMIT receipt ensures the two-phase check-then-act pattern is enforced at the interface level, making it auditable independently of the Policy Engine's internal deny log (SUB-ACG-008).

items.text: The interface between the Adapter Framework and the Allowlist and Configuration Governance Policy Engine SHALL use a synchronous in-process RPC call (ActionPermitRequest → ActionPermitResponse); the request SHALL carry: adapter ID, action class, target resource URI, and requesting intent ID; the Policy Engine SHALL respond within 10 ms with a permit or deny decision; a non-response within 10 ms SHALL be treated by the Adapter Framework as a DENY.
items.rationale: Every action brokered via the Adapter Framework must be authorised against the allowlist before execution (SUB-ACG-007). The synchronous RPC model with a 10 ms hard timeout preserves the 200 ms intent execution budget (SYS-REQ-009) while making the DENY-on-timeout behaviour explicit — a silent timeout that defaults to PERMIT would be a safety bypass for any allowlist policy.

items.text: The interface between the Maintenance Configuration Interface and the Host Core Runtime SHALL receive a mode-change event carrying the new operating mode (Maintenance or non-Maintenance) within 500 ms of the mode transition, enabling the interface to enable or disable allowlist management operations accordingly.
items.rationale: The Maintenance Configuration Interface must gate on host mode to enforce SYS-REQ-005. The 500 ms latency budget for the mode event is generous relative to the mode-transition latency of Host Core Runtime; a longer delay would create a window during which management operations could be accepted in the wrong mode.

items.text: The interface between the Policy Engine and the Remote Action Gateway SHALL transmit a synchronous gate evaluation request containing the SSH command string and target host identity, and SHALL receive an explicit ALLOW or DENY decision prior to SSH dispatch, using an in-process function call.
items.rationale: SYS-REQ-015 requires each remote SSH command to pass through the allowlist gate before dispatch. The interface must carry enough context (command + target) for the Policy Engine to resolve the allowlist entry correctly; a bare command string without target identity would permit allowlisting bypass via host aliasing.

items.text: The interface between the Policy Engine and the Adapter Framework SHALL transmit a synchronous gate evaluation request containing the action identifier and adapter identity, and SHALL receive a binary ALLOW or DENY decision within 5 ms, using an in-process function call over a defined stable API contract.
items.rationale: The Adapter Framework must query the Policy Engine gate before dispatching any external application action (SYS-REQ-004, SYS-REQ-014). An in-process call eliminates IPC serialisation latency on the critical path; a stable API contract ensures future Adapter Framework versions cannot bypass the gate by changing calling conventions.

items.text: **Decision:** Implement Allowlist Gate enforcement in conformance with IEC 62443 (Security for industrial automation and control systems) Part 3-3 SR 2.1 (Authorisation Enforcement); implement SSH Command Gate with operator-transparent rejection responses that identify the denied command class and allowlist reference, ensuring proportionate governance of the command execution boundary. **Rationale:** The Allowlist Gate (hex 40A53950) is Institutionally Defined: IEC 62443-3-3 SR 2.1 is the applicable standard for action-boundary access control in a system integrating external tools. The SSH Command Gate (hex 41E53909) is Ethically Significant: opaque rejections create a disproportionate information asymmetry between the host operator and the adapter developer — structured rejection payloads allow the governance boundary to be understood and contested through legitimate means.
items.rationale: Architecture decision addressing Ontological Mismatch findings for Allowlist Gate (Institutionally Defined without standards reference) and SSH Command Gate (Ethically Significant without ethical/proportionality requirement). IEC 62443 is in the project standards registry; proportionality is the governing ethical principle for access-control gates that can be exercised against third-party developers.

items.text: **Decision:** Govern SSH Audit Logger operation under ISO/IEC 27001 (Information security management systems — Requirements) Annex A control 8.15 (Logging) and apply data-minimisation to audit records: log only command text, target host, adapter identity, allowlist verdict, duration, and result code. Credentials, private key material, and retrieved file content SHALL be excluded from audit records by design. **Rationale:** The SSH Audit Logger (hex 40853349) is classified as Institutionally Defined (governed by information-security standards) and Ethically Significant (it records operator command intent over a privileged channel). Conformance to ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.15 ensures the audit trail is tamper-evident and retained appropriately; data-minimisation limits the ethical risk of disproportionate surveillance on knowledge-worker operations.
items.rationale: Architecture decision addressing the dual Ontological Mismatch findings for SSH Audit Logger: (1) Institutionally Defined without standards reference, (2) Ethically Significant without ethical requirement. ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.15 is in the project standards registry; GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) data-minimisation principle constrains what the logger may collect.

items.text: **Decision:** The desklet link-loss detection and role-restoration requirement (SYS-REQ-008) is implemented across five subsystems — Local Transport Server (heartbeat ping), Desklet Pairing (liveness probe), Host Core Runtime (health watchdog), Desklet Client Framework (disconnection detection and resume), and Knowledge Worker Station (staleness indicator) — because each subsystem manages an independent segment of the desklet lifecycle. **Context:** SYS-REQ-008 is a cross-cutting resilience requirement: no single subsystem owns the full detection-to-restoration chain. **Alternatives considered:** (a) centralising heartbeat logic in a dedicated watchdog subsystem — rejected, would require all subsystems to expose internal liveness state over an IPC channel, adding latency; (b) routing through the Intent Router — rejected, intent path is for user actions, not infrastructure health. **Consequences:** Trace fan-out from SYS-REQ-008 to six SUB requirements across five subsystems is by design. Each link targets a distinct failure-detection or recovery action, not a repeated capability. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record justifying the cross-subsystem distribution of SYS-REQ-008 heartbeat and role-restoration logic. The five-subsystem spread is an intentional cross-cutting resilience pattern, not a decomposition defect. This ARC satisfies the spray-pattern hard-fail gate for SYS-REQ-008 (6 SUB children, threshold 4).

items.text: **Decision:** Decompose Remote SSH Boundary into SSH Session Rate Limiter, SSH Command Gate, SSH Config Resolver, and SSH Audit Logger — with the rate limiter as the entry point before any credential resolution or subprocess spawn. **Context:** SYS-REQ-015 and SYS-REQ-001 together require that all SSH execution occur inside the host process with allowlist checking and operator credential usage, creating a need for a clear sequence: rate-check → allowlist-check → credential-resolve → spawn → audit. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Combine rate limiting and allowlist checking in a single gate — rejected because they have independent update cycles (rate state is ephemeral; allowlist is persisted policy) and different failure modes; (2) Invoke SSH via the Remote Action Gateway's existing Allowlist Gate — rejected because the RAG gate is designed for general actions and SSH needs additional controls (timeout, credential resolution, rate limiting) that would overload it. **Consequences:** SSH credential files are never read until a command has passed both rate-limit and allowlist checks, minimising access to sensitive host files. The SSH Audit Logger has no path to suppress its own write from within the subsystem. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for Remote SSH Boundary; Inspection verifies the rationale and sequencing are documented coherently.

items.text: **Decision:** Implement Deep Integration Boundary as a dedicated subsystem with four components (Deep Adapter Protocol Handler, Event Subscription Manager, Deep Session Tracker, Capability Scope Enforcer) providing bidirectional JSON-RPC over Unix domain socket for DEEP adapters. **Context:** DEEP adapters require event subscriptions and bidirectional communication beyond the one-way HTTP callback model used by BASIC adapters; mixing both modes in the Adapter Framework would couple unrelated lifecycle concerns and complicate the security boundary. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Extend Adapter Framework with DEEP mode support — rejected because DEEP sessions have stateful lifecycle (heartbeat, reconnect grace, subscriptions) that the stateless BASIC dispatch model cannot accommodate cleanly; (2) WebSocket over localhost HTTP — rejected in favour of Unix domain socket to avoid port allocation conflicts and limit attack surface to local processes only. **Consequences:** DEEP adapters must co-locate on the same host as the ContextRail runtime; remote DEEP adapters are not supported by this design. The Capability Scope Enforcer must receive live policy updates from Allowlist and Configuration Governance on every allowlist change. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for Deep Integration Boundary decomposition; Inspection verifies the rationale and design choice are documented and coherent.

items.text: **Decision:** Implement the Desklet Client Framework as four independent in-process components (Desklet Shell Renderer, Lifecycle State Machine, Context Feed Client, Connection Health Monitor) communicating via an in-process event bus. **Context:** The DCF runs on resource-constrained desklet devices (tablets/secondary displays) and must achieve <500ms role render latency and <1s quiesce latency without inter-process serialisation overhead. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Monolithic single-component client — rejected because it couples rendering, lifecycle, and connection concerns, making individual testing and fault isolation impossible; (2) Separate OS processes for each component — rejected because IPC serialisation adds 10-50ms latency per call, consuming a significant fraction of the quiesce budget. **Consequences:** Tight coupling through the shared event bus means a crash in any component crashes the whole client process; the design relies on the host's watchdog to detect and restart the client process within the resumption budget. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record — no EARS SHALL statement required. Verification by inspection of the implemented component structure against this decision.

items.text: **Decision:** Implement Remote Action Gateway as a thin in-process broker — Allowlist Gate → SSH Session Manager → Remote Action Result Emitter — with no persistent connection pool. Each action triggers a fresh SSH session (or reuses an active multiplexed session if the host supports ControlMaster). **Context:** ContextRail executes at most a few concurrent remote actions per desklet; connection pool management complexity is not warranted. The host operator's existing SSH configuration already handles multiplexing and host-key validation. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Persistent SSH connection pool — rejected because session cleanup on LOCK events becomes complex and pool-state diverges from host SSH config changes; (2) Embedded paramiko/libssh2 with its own key management — rejected because SYS-REQ-001 forbids credential storage outside the host; using libssh2 would require importing credential material; (3) Shell exec via child_process.exec — rejected because it provides no structured result (exit code, stdout/stderr) and is vulnerable to shell injection if command arguments are not sanitised. **Consequences:** Per-command session establishment adds ~50–200 ms latency for non-ControlMaster hosts; acceptable for manual action dispatch but unsuitable for high-frequency automation. The 30 s timeout in SSH Session Manager bounds worst-case resource exposure. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Documents the thin-broker architecture decision for the RAG and justifies why a connection pool and alternative SSH implementations were rejected.

items.text: **Decision:** Implement Local Transport Server as a single Node.js process embedding both the HTTP Static Asset Server and WebSocket Gateway on the same port, with in-process Connection Registry, Heartbeat Monitor, and Channel Multiplexer components. **Context:** ContextRail's primary performance constraint is context streaming latency (≤ 5 ms dispatch for 16 desklets at up to 60 Hz); inter-process or network hops between these components would consume the entire budget. The host operator expects a single portable binary with no external dependencies. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Reverse-proxy model with separate nginx + WS process — rejected because reverse-proxy adds ≥ 2 ms per frame on the hot path and complicates TLS certificate sharing; (2) Separate processes for HTTP and WebSocket — rejected because it requires IPC for the upgrade routing handoff and doubles the certificate management surface; (3) Browser WebTransport instead of WebSocket — rejected because browser WebTransport support is not yet universal and desklets run in constrained browser environments. **Consequences:** The single-process design means the HTTP and WebSocket services share the same Node.js event loop; a blocking operation in the static asset server can delay heartbeat processing. Per-socket send queues (in CMX) must be used to prevent head-of-line blocking. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Documents the single-process binding decision for the transport layer. This ADR is the rationale record for why both HTTP serving and WebSocket handling share a process and port, and why IPC alternatives were rejected.

items.text: **Decision:** Decompose Desklet Pairing and Identity into four components: Pairing Token Generator (single-use QR token lifecycle), Device Identity Ledger (persistent pairing records), Role Assignment Manager (role-binding enforcement), and Heartbeat Monitor (liveness detection). **Context:** SYS-REQ-002 requires single-use token authentication; SYS-REQ-007 requires per-desklet role binding; SYS-REQ-008 requires 5 s link-loss detection. Separating identity persistence (DIL) from token generation (PTG) means a compromised token cannot bypass the stored registration — the host still controls which device identities it trusts. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Combined pairing-and-heartbeat daemon — rejected because mixing token lifecycle with periodic monitoring creates a failure-mode coupling: a stalled heartbeat poll could block a pairing handshake under load; (2) OS-level session management — rejected because it would bind ContextRail to a specific OS session mechanism, preventing multi-OS portability implied by the workstation deployment scenario. **Consequences:** The DIL is the single source of trust for registered desklet identities; it must be persisted across restarts. The Heartbeat Monitor must be able to read from DIL without holding its write lock, requiring a read-stable view. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for the Desklet Pairing and Identity decomposition. Documents the four-component design, failure mode isolation, and rejection of OS-session-management coupling. An ADR captures a design choice and its rationale; it is not a functional constraint and does not require a verification method.

items.text: **Decision:** Decompose the Workspace Context Store into three components: Context Object Registry (authoritative state), Role Scope Filter (projection layer), and Event Bus Adapter (ingestion). **Context:** SYS-REQ-011 requires per-role context scoping; SYS-REQ-012 requires a single authoritative context model; SYS-REQ-003 requires streaming to cease on lock. Separating storage from projection avoids coupling the lock gate (which only needs to pause streaming) to the storage layer. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Monolithic context store — rejected because mixing ingestion, storage, and projection in one component makes the Locked-state streaming suspension harder to enforce and harder to test independently; (2) Per-role stores — rejected because authoritative state would be fragmented and require reconciliation on role reassignment, contradicting SYS-REQ-012. **Consequences:** The Role Scope Filter becomes the single choke point for enforcing per-role information boundaries; its filter logic must be verified per role. The Event Bus Adapter is the only write path into the registry, making audit straightforward. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for the Workspace Context Store decomposition. Documents the three-component design choice, the alternatives considered (monolithic store, per-role stores), and the engineering consequences. An ADR does not carry a verification method — it is a design record, not a functional constraint.

items.text: **Decision:** The Workspace Executor decomposes into three sequential components — Command Dispatcher, Process Supervisor, and Outcome Reporter — rather than a monolithic execution engine or a two-stage dispatch/report split. **Context:** The 200 ms p95 intent-to-outcome latency budget (SYS-REQ-009) requires clear stage boundaries so each stage can be instrumented and budgeted independently; the audit and context-update requirements (IFC-EXE-017, IFC-EXE-018) require intent-ID correlation to be preserved from receipt through to outcome publication. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Monolithic executor — rejected because a single component mixes validation, process management, and event publication, making it impossible to attribute latency to a specific stage or test stages in isolation. (2) Two-stage (Dispatcher + Outcome) — rejected because subprocess lifecycle management (timeout enforcement, SIGKILL, stdout capture) is substantively different from routing and deserves an isolated failure domain; a hung subprocess must not block the Dispatcher from accepting the next intent. **Consequences:** Three components must share in-process state for the CommandEnvelope and ProcessResult; this is acceptable as all three run in the same host process and no cross-process serialisation is required. The Process Supervisor becomes the single point of timeout enforcement and must be tested with injected hung-process scenarios. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture Decision Record for Workspace Executor decomposition. An ADR is a design record, not a binding requirement; the rationale field carries the engineering context: the three-component split was chosen to isolate distinct failure domains (routing, subprocess management, outcome publication) and to make per-stage latency instrumentation feasible against the SYS-REQ-009 200ms budget.

items.text: **Decision:** Decompose Host Core Runtime into four components: Configuration Loader, Startup Orchestrator, Health Watchdog, and Mode State Machine. **Context:** SYS-REQ-013 requires automatic degraded-mode operation when a subsystem fails, necessitating clean separation of failure detection from mode governance. **Alternatives considered:** Monolithic host-controller rejected — single component cannot independently test health-to-mode transition chain. Embedded heartbeat monitor rejected — coupling health polling to the main event loop violates the 2s detection budget under load. **Consequences:** Startup Orchestrator must maintain the boot dependency order; any new subsystem requires an update to its sequence. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: ISO/IEC/IEEE 25010 single-responsibility decomposition principle. Separating health detection from mode governance prevents a detection bug from corrupting mode state, enabling independent subsystem verification.

items.text:
**Decision:** Decompose the Intent Router (INT) into three in-process components: Intent Dispatcher (IDISP), Conflict Serialiser (CSER), and Dispatch Confirmer (DCONF).

**Context:** SYS-REQ-009 mandates a 200 ms round-trip latency budget for intent dispatch confirmation. SYS-REQ-010 requires that concurrent intents targeting the same workspace context object be serialised without data races. SYS-REQ-007 requires that each intent carry a role-validated permission record before dispatch. A monolithic Intent Router cannot independently evolve latency-critical routing, conflict ordering, and confirmation tracking — each has distinct state and timing concerns.

**Alternatives considered:** (1) Single-component router with inline queue — rejected because combining serialisation logic with dispatch and confirmation tracking makes latency budgets difficult to verify independently. (2) Separate process per component — rejected because the added IPC overhead (>10 ms per hop) risks breaching the 200 ms budget.

**Consequences:** IDISP handles ACG gate check and routing; CSER owns the per-context-object serialisation queue with explicit SUPERSEDED status emission; DCONF tracks execution outcomes and delivers confirmations within the latency window, emitting TIMEOUT if no acknowledgment is received. All three communicate synchronously in-process via direct function call, keeping internal latency under 5 ms per hop.

**Status:** Accepted
items.rationale: Three distinct responsibilities (role-gated routing, conflict serialisation, confirmation tracking) with different state and timing requirements are best expressed as separate, independently-testable components; in-process coupling preserves the 200 ms latency budget.

items.text: **Decision:** The Allowlist and Configuration Governance subsystem decomposes into four components: Policy Engine (default-deny gate evaluated synchronously on every external action), Allowlist Store (persistent host-side key-value store of permitted actions), Maintenance Configuration Interface (host-only management surface, active only in Maintenance mode), and Allowlist Audit Logger (append-only structured log of every gate decision). **Context:** SYS-REQ-004 requires every host-mediated action to be evaluated against an allowlist with deny-and-log semantics; SYS-REQ-005 requires that allowlist management be exclusively host-side and mode-gated. The gate must be synchronous and non-bypassable. **Alternatives considered:** (1) Inline allowlist check inside each caller subsystem — rejected because enforcement becomes distributed and a future subsystem addition could omit it; a single mandatory gate is the only reliable default-deny architecture. (2) Persistent process with IPC — rejected in favour of in-process Policy Engine to eliminate IPC round-trip latency on the critical action path. (3) Remote policy service — rejected because SYS-REQ-016 prohibits outbound internet connectivity and all functions must operate offline. **Consequences:** Every action-dispatching subsystem (Adapter Framework, Remote Action Gateway) must pass through the Policy Engine gate before dispatch; bypassing it is architecturally impossible without modifying ACG. The audit log grows unboundedly and requires a host-side retention policy outside ContextRail's scope. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: ARC record of the decomposition rationale for ACG. No numeric acceptance criteria — this is a design decision, not a testable assertion.

items.text: **Decision:** The Security and Lock Manager decomposes into four components: Pairing Token Authority (single-use token issuance/validation), Host Authenticator (OS PAM integration and lock event detection), Lock State Controller (canonical lock state machine and quiesce broadcast), and Context Access Guard (role-scoped filtering and credential-leakage prevention). **Context:** SYS-REQ-001 through SYS-REQ-003 and SYS-REQ-011 impose distinct security obligations — credential isolation, device authentication, sub-1-second lock quiesce, and role-scoped context streaming — that map to orthogonal failure modes and test surfaces. Collapsing them into a single component would make the 1s lock latency unverifiable and credential-isolation testable only end-to-end. **Alternatives considered:** (a) Two-component split (auth + context guard): rejected because it conflates token lifecycle management with OS session events, making the 60s token expiry and the lock event path share state incorrectly; (b) Single monolithic security module: rejected because mixing pairing logic with lock-state broadcast requires synchronous locking that would violate the 1s quiesce budget under load. **Consequences:** Requires well-defined internal interfaces between HAUTH→LSC (lock event), LSC→CAG (state gate), and PTA→LSC (auth status); the Context Access Guard sits on the hot path for every context stream event. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision for SLM decomposition: four-component split isolates orthogonal failure modes (credential storage, OS authentication, lock timing, context filtering) enabling independent verification of the 60s token expiry, 1s lock quiesce, and role-scope enforcement obligations.

items.text: **Decision:** Locked is the defined confidentiality safe state — context streaming and intent execution cease within 1 s while the allowlist boundary stays enforced, until host re-authentication. **Context:** An unattended workstation must collapse streamed-context confidentiality to a known state. **Alternatives considered:** (a) idle timeout that merely blanks desklets — rejected, context still resident/streamable; (b) full host shutdown — rejected, destroys session continuity. **Consequences:** sub-second confidentiality collapse with recoverable session; allowlist enforcement is independent of lock state. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for the Locked confidentiality safe state.

items.text: **Decision:** A single host-authoritative workspace context model with receipt-order serialisation of conflicting intents and role-scoped streaming to desklets. **Context:** OQ-02 — concurrent action by multiple desklets on shared context was unspecified. **Alternatives considered:** (a) per-desklet local context caches with merge — rejected, divergence and conflict complexity; (b) last-writer-wins without notification — rejected, silent loss of superseded intents. **Consequences:** deterministic consistency; superseded intents reported; host is the consistency authority. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record resolving OQ-02 concurrency/consistency.

items.text: **Decision:** Desklet trust is established by a single-use, 60 s bounded-lifetime pairing token bound to the device for the session. **Context:** OQ-01 — source pack left the pairing trust model unspecified, but it is material to the security boundary. **Alternatives considered:** (a) open join by URL/QR with no auth — rejected, any QR observer joins; (b) persistent device certificates — rejected, adds state to zero-state desklets and a revocation burden. **Consequences:** captured QR/URL has a bounded replay window; re-pairing needed across sessions; no long-lived secret on the device. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record resolving OQ-01 pairing trust model.

items.text: **Decision:** All host-mediated external and remote actions pass through a single default-deny allowlist gate. **Context:** STK-REQ-005/STK-REQ-009 and OQ-04 require a bounded, owner-governed action surface. **Alternatives considered:** (a) per-adapter ad-hoc checks — rejected, no single enforceable boundary; (b) allow-by-default with blocklist — rejected, fails closed only by omission. **Consequences:** one auditable chokepoint for BASIC/DEEP/SSH actions; adapters cannot bypass it; allowlist edits confined to host Maintenance mode. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for the single default-deny allowlist gate.

items.text: **Decision:** Desklets are browser/PWA clients requiring no native install. **Context:** Platform constraint — heterogeneous user-owned spare devices, zero-friction repurposing. **Alternatives considered:** (a) native per-OS apps — rejected, install friction defeats the spare-device premise and widens the attack surface; (b) remote-desktop streaming — rejected, heavy and not role-scoped. **Consequences:** UI limited to web runtime capabilities; transport is WebSocket over local HTTP; cross-device consistency handled host-side. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for the zero-state web/PWA desklet client model.

items.text: **Decision:** The host process is the sole point of execution, credential storage, and network access; desklets are zero-state render/dispatch surfaces. **Context:** INV-02/INV-03 require credentials and execution to never leave the workstation. **Alternatives considered:** (a) desklet-side execution — rejected, leaks capability onto user devices; (b) cloud automation broker — rejected, reintroduces third-party execution exposure. **Consequences:** all latency-sensitive paths route through the host; desklets are cheap and disposable; host availability becomes the single point of failure, mitigated by Degraded mode. **Status:** Accepted.
items.rationale: Architecture decision record for the host-sole-execution topology underpinning the confidentiality boundary.
